names.japanese_name: フェロシアン化カルシウム

natural_occurrence.description: Calcium ferrocyanide does not occur naturally. It is synthetically manufactured for use as an anti-caking agent in salt.

manufacturing.description: Produced by reacting sodium or potassium ferrocyanide with calcium chloride. Like other ferrocyanides, the hexacyanoferrate complex is extremely stable with cyanide groups tightly bound to iron.

other_uses.industrial: Limited industrial use compared to sodium and potassium ferrocyanides.

eu.conditions: Restricted to use in salt and salt substitutes only. Same maximum levels as other ferrocyanides.

usa.notes: Limited to 13 ppm in table salt.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: フェロシアン化カルシウム

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0-0.025 mg/kg bw/day (as ferrocyanide ion, group ADI for all ferrocyanides)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 0.03 mg/kg bw/day (as ferrocyanide ion, group ADI)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA evaluated all three ferrocyanides (E535, E536, E538) together and established a group ADI.

fun_facts: Third member of the ferrocyanide family - all contain CYANIDE but are SAFE due to stable iron complex
Less commonly used than sodium (E535) or potassium (E536) ferrocyanides
Provides calcium along with anti-caking properties, though the calcium content is negligible
All three ferrocyanides (E535, E536, E538) work the same way and have the same safety profile
The choice between sodium, potassium, or calcium ferrocyanide depends on the salt formulation and desired mineral profile
Despite alarming name with 'cyanide', these have been used safely in salt for decades worldwide
